`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nJason Company, a Philippine Corporation, sold inventory on credit to a Japanese Company on April 8, 2020. Jason received payment of 35,000 yens on May 8, 2020. The exchange rate was P1 = Y0.65 on April 8 and P1 = Y0.70 on May 8. What amount of foreign exchange gain or loss should be recognized? (rounded to the nearest peso) (Foreign Currency Translation)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP10,500 loss`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP10,500 gain`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP1,750 loss`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP3,846 loss`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nJane had the following foreign currencies transactions during 2020:`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nPurchased merchandise from a foreign supplier on January 20, 2020, for the Philippine peso equivalent to P60,000 and paid the invoice on April 20, 2020, at the Philippine peso equivalent to P68,000.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nOn September 1, 2020, borrowed the Philippine peso equivalent of P300,000 evidenced by a note that is payable in the lender’s local currency on September 1, 2021. On December 31, 2020, the Philippine peso equivalent of the principal amount of P320,000.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nIn Jane’s 2020 income statement, what amount should be included is a foreign exchange loss? (Foreign Currency Translation)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n4,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n20,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n22,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n28,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nAssuming that Joshua entered into no forward contract, how much foreign exchange gain or loss it reports on its 2020 income statement about this transaction? (Foreign Currency Translation - Hedging)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP5,000 gain`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP3,000 gain`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP2,000 loss`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP1,000 loss`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nAssuming that Joshua entered into a forward contract to sell 10 million foreign currencies on December 1, 2020, as a fair value of hedge of foreign currency receivable, what is the net impact on its net income in 2020 resulting from a fluctuation in the value of the foreign currencies? (Foreign Currency Translation - Hedging)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nNo impact in net income`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP58.80 decrease in net income`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP2,000 decrease in net income`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP1,941.20 increase in net income`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nPNO Corporation subsidiary buys marketable equity securities and inventory on April 1, 2020, for 100,000 foreign currencies each. It pays for both items on June 1, 2020, and they are still on hand at year-end. Inventory is carried at a cost under the lower-of-cost-or market value. Currency exchange rates for 1 peso follow:`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nJanuary 1, 20200.15`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nApril 1, 20200.16`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nJune 1, 20200.17`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nDecember 31, 20200.19`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nAssume that the foreign currency is the subsidiary’s functional currency. What balances does a consolidated balance sheet report as of December 31, 2020? (Foreign Currency Translation)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nMarketable equity securities (P16,000); Inventory (P16,000)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nMarketable equity securities (P17,000); Inventory (P17,000)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nMarketable equity securities (P19,000); Inventory (P16,000)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nMarketable equity securities (P19,000); Inventory (P19,000)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nAly Corporation subsidiary buys marketable equity securities and inventory on April 1, 2020, for 100,000 foreign currencies each. It pays for both items on June 1, 2020, and they are still on hand at year-end. Inventory is carried at a cost under the lower-of-cost-or market value. Currency exchange rates for 1 peso follow:`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nJanuary 1, 20200.15`r`n`r`n
`r`nApril 1, 20200.16`r`n`r`n
`r`nJune 1, 20200.17`r`n`r`n
`r`nDecember 31, 20200.19`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`nAssume that the peso is the subsidiary’s functional currency. What balances does a consolidated balance sheet report as of December 31, 2020? (Foreign Currency Translation)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nMarketable equity securities (P16,000); Inventory (P16,000)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nMarketable equity securities (P17,000); Inventory (P17,000)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nMarketable equity securities (P19,000); Inventory (P16,000)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nMarketable equity securities (P19,000); Inventory (P19,000)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nOn January 1, 2020, Leonardo Co. sold equipment to its subsidiary, Bernie Corp., for P115,000. The equipment had cost P 125,000, and the balance in accumulated depreciation was P45,000. The equipment has estimated remaining useful life of eight years and no salvage value. Both companies use straight-line depreciation. On their separate 2014 income statements, Leonardo and Bernie reported depreciation expense of P84,000 and P60,000, respectively. The amount of depreciation expense on the consolidated income statement for 2020 would have been (Property, Plant and Equipment - Depreciation)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n144,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n148,375`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n139,625`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n134,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nWhat is the remeasurement gain to be recognized on the consolidated income statement? (Property, Plant and Equipment - Remeasurement)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n2,100`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n2,400`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n2,700`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n3,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nWhich of the following items is not remeasurement using historical exchange rate under the temporal or remeasurement method? (Property, Plant and Equipment - Remeasurement)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nAccumulated depreciation on equipment`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nCost of goods sold`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nMarketable equity securities`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nRetained earnings`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nGeorge Corporation starts a subsidiary in a foreign country; the subsidiary's functional currency is not the currency of a hyperinflationary economy (using the current rate method). On January 1, George buys all of the subsidiary's common stock for 20,000 foreign currencies (FC). On April 1, the subsidiary purchases inventory for 20,000 FCs with payment made on May 1, and sells this inventory on August 1, for 30,000 FCs, which it collects on October 1. Currency exchange rates for one foreign currency are as follows:`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nJanuary 1 .15`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nApril 1 .17`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nMay 1 .18`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nAugust 1 .19`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nOctober 1 .20`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nDecember 1 .21`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`nIn preparing consolidated financial statements, what translation adjustment will George report at the end of the current year? (Foreign Currency Transactions)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP400 positive (credit)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP600 positive (credit)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP1,400 positive (credit)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP1,800 positive (credit)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nOn April 1, Quality Corporation, a Philippine Company, expects to order merchandise from a foreign supplier in three-months, denominating the transaction in foreign currencies, On April 1, the exchange rate is P 1.19 per foreign currency and Quality enters into a three-month forward contract to purchase 400,000 foreign currencies at a rate of 1.20. At the end of three months, three months, the exchange rate is P 1.21 per euro and Quality orders and receives the merchandise, paying 400,000 foreign currencies. What are the effects on net income from these transactions? (Foreign Currency Transactions)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP4,000 Discount Expense plus a P4,000 negative Adjustment to Net income when merchandise is received`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP4,000 Discount Expense plus a P8,000 negative Adjustment to Net income when merchandise is received`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP4,000 Premium Expense plus a P4,000 negative Adjustment to Net income when merchandise is received`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP8,000 Premium Expense plus a P4,000 positive Adjustment to Net income when merchandise is received`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP8,000 Premium Expense plus a P8,000 positive Adjustment to Net income when merchandise is received`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nKaren Inc., a Philippine Company, expects to order goods from a foreign supplier for 100,000 foreign currencies, with delivery and payment to be made on April 17. On January 17, Karen purchased a three-month call option on 100,000 foreign currencies and designated this. Option as a cash flow hedge of a forecasted foreign currency transaction. The following exchange rates apply: `r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nOption strike price 4.34`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nOption cost5,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nJuly 24 exchange rate 4.34`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nApril 17 exchange rate 4.26`r`n`r`n
`r`nWhat amount will Karen include as an option expense in net income during the period January 17 to April 17? (Foreign Currency Transactions)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n4,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n4,260`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n4,340`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n5,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nMishiel Company purchases raw material from its foreign supplier, Bolivian Clay, on May 8. Payment of 2,000,000 foreign currency- units (FC) is due in 30 days. May 31 is Brisco’s fiscal year-end. The pertinent exchange rates were as follows:`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`nMay 81.25`r`n`r`n
`r`nMay 311.26`r`n`r`n
`r`nJune 71.20`r`n`r`n
`r`nHow much foreign exchange gain or loss should Brisco record on May 31? (Foreign Currency Transactions)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP2,520,000 gain`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP20,000 gain`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP20,000 loss`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP80,000 gain`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nJane, Inc., a Philippine Company, has a Japanese yen account receivable resulting from an export sale on March 1 to a customer in Japan. The exporter signed a forward contract on March 1 to sell yen and designated it’s as a cash flow hedge of a recognized receivable. The exchange rate was P0.0094 and the forward rate P0.0093. Which of the following did the Philippines exporter report in net income? (Foreign Currency Transactions)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nDiscount revenue`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nPremium revenue`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nDiscount expense`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nPremium expense`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nThe entries on December 31, 2020, include a (Foreign Currency Transactions)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nCredit to Foreign Currency Payable to Exchange Broker, P4,0000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nDebit to Foreign Currency Receivable from Exchange Broker, P6,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nDebit to Foreign Currency Receivable from Exchange Broker, P186,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nDebit Foreign Currency Transaction Gain, P4,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nThe entries on January 30, 2021, include a (Foreign Currency Transactions)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nDebit a Pesos Payable to Exchange Broker, P180,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nCredit to Cash, 184,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nCredit to Premium on Forward Contract, P4,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nCredit to Foreign Currency Receivable from Exchange Broker, P180,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nThe entries on January 30, 2021, include a (Foreign Currency Transactions)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nCredit a Foreign Currency Units (Bahts), P 184,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nCredit to Cash, P 184,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nDebit to Foreign Currency transaction Loss, P4,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nDebit to Pesos Payable to Exchange Broker, P184,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nThe entries on January 30, 2020, include a (Foreign Currency Transactions)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nDebit to Pesos Payable to Exchange Broker, P184,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nCredit to foreign Currency Transaction Gain, P4,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nCredit to Foreign Currency Receivable from Exchange Broker, P180,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nDebit to Foreign Currency Units (Bahts), P 184,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nThe present value of the average excess earnings over the next four years discounted at 15%. (The present value of an ordinary annuity of I at I 5% for four periods is 2.85498) (Effective Interest Rates)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n31,792,979`r`n`r`n

`r`n`r`n`r`nIf Jun were to pay P23,100,000 to purchase the assets and assume the liabilities of Sayo Na Co., how much would be charged to goodwill? (Business Combination – Goodwill)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n8,840,000`r`n`r`n

`r`n`r`n`r`nA company is considering purchasing another company, which has the following assets and liabilities.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n Cost Fair Market Value `r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nAccounts receivable P 4,800,000 P 4,400,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nInventory 4,800,000 5,000,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nPrepaid Insurance 200,000 200,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nBuildings and equipment (net) 1,400,000 4,000,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nAccounts payable (3,200,000) (3,200,000)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n Net assets P8,000,000 P 10,400,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nIf the purchase price is 12,600,000, the amount of goodwill to be charged in recording the acquisition is (Business Combination – Goodwill)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n4,600,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n2,400,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n2,200,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n0`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nOn April 1, 2020, AV Corp. acquired 80% of the outstanding stocks of SR Corp. for P2,500,000. SR Corp.'s stockholders' equity at the end of 2020 is as follows: Common stock, P80 par P2, 000,000, additional paid-in capital P500,000, and Retained Earnings P750,000. The fair value of the non- controlling interest is P685,000. All the assets of SR were fairly valued except for its inventories, which are undervalued by P90,000, Land, which is undervalued by P50,000, and the Patent, which is undervalued by P125,000. The said patent has a remaining useful life of five years. Both companies use the straight-line method for depreciation and amortization. Shareholders' equity of AV Corp. on December 31, 2020, is composed of Common stock, P50 par P3,500,000, APIC P750,000, and retained earnings P2,460,000. Goodwill, if any, should be decreased by P22,500 every year-end. No additional issuance of capital stocks occurred.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`nConsolidated shareholders' equity (Business Combination)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n7,419,150`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n7,893,750`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n7,112,600`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n7,865,750`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nThe Antony Company acquired a 60% interest in the Jeano Company when Jeano's equity comprised share capital of P100,000 and retained earnings of P150,000. Jeano's current statement of financial position shows share capital of P100,000, a revaluation reserve of P75,000 and retained earnings of P300,000.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nUnder PAS27 Consolidated and separate financial statements, what figure in respect of the noncontrolling interest should be included in Antony's consolidated statement of financial position? (Business Combination)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n90,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n150,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n160,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n190,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nOn October 1, 2020, The Regan Company acquired 100% of the Elijah Company when the fair value of Elijah's net assets was P116 million, and its carrying amount was P120 million. The consideration transferred comprised P200 million in cash transferred at the acquisition date, plus another P60 million in cash to be transferred 11 months after the acquisition date, if a specified profit target was met by Elijah. At the acquisition date, there was only a low probability of the profit target being met, so the fair value of the additional consideration liability was P10 million. In the event, the profit target was met, and the P60 million cash was transferred.`r`n`r`n
`r`nWhat amount should Regan present for goodwill in its statement of consolidated financial position at December 31, 2021, according to PFRS 3 Business combinations? (Business Combination)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n80,000,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n84,000,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n94,000,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n144,000,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n100% of the equity share capital of the Richmond Company was acquired by The Janice Company on June 30, 2020. Janice issued 500,000 new P1 ordinary shares, which had a fair value of P8 of each at the acquisition date. Also, the acquisition resulted in Janice incurring fees payable to external advisers of P200,000 and share issue costs of P180,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nBy PFRS 3 Business Combinations, goodwill at the acquisition gate is measured by subtracting the identifiable assets acquired and the liabilities assumed from: (Business Combination)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n4,000,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n4,180,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n4,200,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n4,390,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nJason Co. acquired inventories on May 1, 2020, from its 70% owned subsidiary, Paz Company. The inventories were sold for P94,000, including the 25% markup on cost. Out of these inventories, 65% were sold to outsiders. During the year, Jason reported a net income of P215,000, and Paz reported a net income of P 140,000.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nHow much is the realized profit to be allocated to non controlling interest in 2021? (Business Combination)`r`n`r`n

`r`n`r`n`r`nThe Sylvia Company acquired a 70% interest in The Clarke Company for P1,420,000 when the fair value of Clarke's identifiable assets and liabilities was P1,200,000. Sylvia acquired a 65% interest in The Homer Company for P300,000 when the fair value of Homer's identifiable assets and liabilities was P640,000. Sylvia measures non-controlling interest at the relevant share if the identifiable net assets at the acquisition date. Neither Clarke nor Homer has any contingent liabilities at the amounts in their financial statements. Annual impairment reviews have not resulted in any impairment losses being recognized. `r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nUnder PFRS 3 Business Combinations, what is the goodwill that should be included in Sylvia's consolidated statement of financial position? (Business Combination)`r`n`r`n

`r`n`r`n`r`nThe Brown Company acquired 80% of the Ayannah Company for a consideration transferred to P100 million. The consideration was estimated to include a control premium of P24 million, Ayannah's net assets were P85 million at the acquisition date. Are the following statements true or false, according to PFRS 3 Business Combinations? (Business Combination)`r`n`r`n

`r`n`r`n`r`nTrue, False`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nTrue, True`r`n`r`n

`r`n`r`n`r`nOn October 1, 2013, The Fernando Company acquired I 00% of The Austria Coompany when the fair value of Austria's net assets was P16 million, and their carrying amount was P120 million, The consideration transferred comprised P200 million in cash transferred at the acquisition date, plus another P60 million in cash to be transferred 11 months after the acquisition date if a specified profit target was met by Austria. `r`n`r`n`r`nPSY Corporation owns 90%, of the outstanding common shares of SVG Company. On January 2, 2020, office equipment that had a carrying value to SVG Company P480,000 and has a remaining life of 10 years was sold to PSY Corporation for P400,00. On the other hand, last August 31, 2021, PSY Corporation sole a second-hand delivery van to SVO Company at a gain of P30,000 (remaining life of 5 years). `r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nIncluded in the January 1, 2021 inventory of PSY Company was merchandise inventory worth P65,000, while SVG Company had P80,000 on its December 31, 2021. These inventories came from inter-company sales and purchases. PSY Corporation included a mark-up of 25% on cost while SVG Company charged a 30% mark-up on sales. `r`n`r`n`r`nThe Joanne Company owns 65% of The Mary Company. On December 31, 2020, the last day of the accounting period, Joanne sold Mary a noncurrent asset for P1,000. The asset's original cost was P2,500, and on December 31, 2020, Its carrying amount in Joanne's book was P800. The group's consolidated statement of financial position has been drafted without any adjustments about this non-current asset.`r`n`r`n

`r`n`r`n`r`nThe Conrad Company acquired equipment on January 1, 2020, at the cost of P800,000, depreciating it over eight years with a nil residual value. `r`n`r`n`r`nIn 2020, Jerico Construction Company was contracted to do a private road network of Philip Corporation for P50 million. The project was estimated to be completed in two years.`r`n`r`n

`r`n`r`n`r`nThe construction contract provided the number of other things the following:`r`n`r`n

`r`n`r`n`r`n5% mobilization fee (to be deducted from the last billing) payable within 15 days after the signing of the contract.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nRetention provision of 10% on all billings.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nProgress billings on construction are payable within seven days from date of acceptance.`r`n`r`n

`r`n`r`n`r`nOn January 2, 2020: Magnolia Ice Cream signed an agreement authorizing Trisha to operate as a franchisee for an initial franchise fee P500,000 received upon signing of the agreement. Trisha commenced operations on August 1, 2020, at which date all of the initial services required of Magnolia Ice Cream had been performed at the cost of P 120,000. The franchise agreement further provides that Trisha must pay a 10% monthly continuing franchising fee. Sales reported from August 1 to December 31, 2020, amount to P400,000.`r`n`r`n

`r`n`r`n`r`nAna's Inc. granted a franchise to Mocca for the Makati area. The franchisee was to pay a franchisee of P500,000, payable in five equal annual installments starting with the payment upon signing of the agreement. The franchise was to pay monthly 3% of gross sales of the preceding month. Should the operations of the outlet prove to be unprofitable, the franchise may be canceled with whatever obligations owing Ana's, Inc. in connection with the P500,000 franchise fee waived. The prevailing interest rate is 14%. The first year generated gross sales of P2,500,000.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n

`r`n`r`n`r`nOn January 2, 2020, Mycor's Inc. signed an agreement to operate as a franchisee of Mang Inasal for an initial franchise fee of P2,343,750 for ten years. Of this amount, P468,750 was paid when the agreement was signed and the balance payable in three annual payments beginning on Dec ember 31, 2020. Mycor's signed a non-interest bearing note for the balance. The implicit interest rate is 18%. Assume that substantial services amounting to P730,000 had already been rendered by the franchisor, and indirect costs of P53,750 have also been incurred. `r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n

`r`n`r`n`r`nOn .January 2, 2020, Diversified Enterprises signed a franchise agreement with DTSI Company for an initial franchise fee of P92,500. Of this amount, DTSI paid P17,500 upon the signing of the franchise contract, and the balance is payable in four annual payments of P18,750 starting December 31, 2020. DTS I issued 12% interest-bearing notes for the balance. The collection of notes is not reasonably assured. `r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nThe down payment is not refundable; it represents the actual cost of initial services, provided by Diversified before formal signing. However, substantial additional services have yet to be performed by Diversified. During 2020 additional direct franchise costs of P45,750 and indirect cost of P15,000 were incurred by Diversified.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nIt is also agreed that DTSI will pay a continuing franchise fee at 3% of its gross sales revenue. The franchise outlet commenced business operations on October 1, 2020, and its gross sales totaled, P600,000 by year-end. `r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nThe net income recognized by Diversified from the DTSI franchise in 2020 is (Franchise Accounting)`r`n`r`n

`r`n`r`n`r`nJodee Construction's Enterprises, a franchisor, charges franchisees a “franchise fee” of P500,000. Of this amount a non-refundable P200,000 is paid upon the signing of the contract with the balance payable in three equal installments after each year after that. Jodee Construction will assist in locating a suitable business site, conduct a market study, oversee the construction of facilities, and provides initial training for employees.`r`n`r`n

`r`n`r`n`r`nInstallment contracts receivable, 2020P60,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n Installment contracts receivable, 2021 192,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nDeferred gross profit, 2020 25,200`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nDeferred gross profit, 202172,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nAt the end of 2022, account balances before adjustment for realized gross profit on installment sales are:`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nInstallment contracts receivable, 2020 P-0-`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nInstallment contracts receivable, 2021 P48,000`r`n`r`n

`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nInstallment sales in 2022 are made at 25% above the cost of merchandise sold. During 2022 upon default in payment by the customer, the company repossessed the merchandise with an estimated market value of P4,000. The sales were in 2021 for P21,600, and P 12,800 had been collected before repossession.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nThe realized gross profit on December 31, 2022 is (Installment Sales)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n147,200 `r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n143,900`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n68,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n140,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`nCesar Company holds 90% of the common stock of Diane Company During 2020, Cesar reported sales of P1,120,000 and cost of goods sold of P 840,000. For this same period, Diane has sales of P 420,000 and cost of goods sold of P 252,000. Also, during 2020, Cesar sold merchandise to Diane for P 140,000. The subsidiary still possesses 40% of this inventory at the end of 2020.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nThe new profit and loss ratio of Michael, Gabriel, Raphael, and Joshua, respectively for 2020 is (Installment Sales)`r`n`r`n

`r`n`r`n`r`n50%; 20%; 10%; and 20%`r`n`r`n

`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n

`r`n`r`n`r`nThe Home Office in Makati shipped merchandise costing P80,000 to the Manila branch and paid for the freight charges of P600. The home office bills the branch at 125% of the cost. Manila branch was subsequently instructed to transfer one-half of the merchandise to Quezon City branch, wherein Quezon City branch paid for P200 freight. If the shipment were made directly from Makati to Quezon City, the freight cost would have been P400. By how much will Manila branch charge the Home Office account? (Home Office and Branch Accounting)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n50,300`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n50,600`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n51,300`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n50,500`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nThe following transactions were incurred for the year by the Company:`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nTransfer of P13,000 merchandise to an agency to establish a working fund.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nReceipt of sales orders from the agency, P130,000.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nCollection of agency accounts by the home office, P91,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nHome office disbursements are representing agency expenses, P11,700.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nReplenishment of the agency working fund upon receipt of expense vouchers for P5,850.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nThe cost of goods sold identified with agency sales, P93,600.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`nHow much is the net income traceable to the agency? (Home Office and Branch Accounting)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n5,850`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n18,850`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n36,400`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n(72,150)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nOn December 31, 2017, the home office of Trisha Supply Company recorded a shipment of merchandise to its Glenda branch as follows:`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nGlenda branchP30,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nShipments to Glenda branch 25,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nUnrealized profit in Glenda branch inventory 4,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nCash (for freight charges) 1,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nThe Glenda branch sells 40% of the merchandise to outside entities during the rest of December 31, 2020. The books of the home office and Trisha branches are closed on December 30 of each year. On January 5, 2021, the Glenda branch transferred half of the original shipments to the Sandy branch, and the Glenda branch pays P500 freight on the shipment. `r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nAt what amounts should the 60% of the merchandise remaining unsold on December 31, 2020, be included in the inventory of the Glenda branch on December 31, 2020? (Home Office and Branch Accounting)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n15,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n17,400`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n15,600`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n18,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nThe Rommel Company was organized in 2015. Shortly after opening its doors to the public at the main store, Rommel Company established a branch in another city. At the end of the second year of operations, the home office received the following condensed income statement from the branch:`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nRevenues P280,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nCost of goods sold 220,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nGross margin P60,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nSelling and administrative expenses 50,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nNet income P10,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nThe management at the home office questioned the accuracy of these figures and assigned you the task of verifying the branch data. Your review of the records uncovered the following facts:`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nThe beginning of year balance in unrealized profit to the branch was P12,000.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nDuring the period, the home office shipped goods to the branch that had cost the main store P150,000. However, your review of the branch receiving reports revealed that several shipments from the home office had been recorded twice by the branch accountant.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nThe branch is billed a uniform 25% above cost and receives inventory only from the home office.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nThe branch ending inventory was correctly reported at a billed price of P43,500. `r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nWhen reconciling reciprocal accounts, you found that the branch had not recorded P4,000 of services performed by the Home Office and billed to the branch. All other selling and administrative expenses were correctly reported by the branch.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nCompute the correct net income of the branch. (Home Office and Branch Accounting)`r`n`r`n
`r`na. 62,800`r`n`r`n
`r`nb. 66,800`r`n`r`n
`r`nc. 50,800`r`n`r`n
`r`nd. 22,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n

`r`nYour audit disclosed the following data:`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nOn December 23, the branch office manager purchased P400,000 of furniture and fixtures failed to notify the home office. The bookkeeper knowing that all fixed assets are carried on the home office, recorded the proper entry on the branch office records. It is the Company’s policy not to take any depreciation assets acquired in the last half of a year.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nOn December 27, a branch office customer erroneously paid his account of P200,000 to the home office. The bookkeeper made the correct entry on the home office books but did not notify the branch office.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nOn December 30, the branch office remitted cash of P500,000, which was received the home office in January 2021`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nOn December 3, the branch office erroneously recorded the December allocated expenses from the home office as P50,000 instead of P150,000. `r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nOn December 31, the home office shipped merchandise billed at P300,000 to the branch office, which was received in January 2020. `r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nThe entire opening inventory of the branch office had been purchased from the home office, Home office 2020 shipments to the branch offices were purchased by the home office in 2020. The physical inventories at December 31, 2020, excluding the shipment in transit, are:`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`nHome office 5,500,000 (at cost)`r`n`r`n
`r`nBranch office 2,000,000 (comprised of P 1,800,000 from home office and `r`n`r`n
`r`n P200,000 from outside vendors)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n7. The home office consistently bills shipments to the branch office at 20% above cost. The sales account is credited for the invoice price.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`nHow much is the correct net income of the branch? (Home Office and Branch Accounting)`r`n`r`n
`r`na. 2,200,000`r`n`r`n
`r`nb. 2,100,000`r`n`r`n
`r`nc. 2,340,000`r`n`r`n
`r`nd. 2,240,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n

`r`n`r`n`r`n Hsensi Company sells goods for cash, on normal credit (2/10, n/30). However, on July 1, 2020, the Company sold a used computer for P22,000; the inventory carrying value was P4,400. The company collected P2,000 cash and agreed to let the customer make payments on the P20,000 whenever possible during the next 12 months. The company management stated that it had no reliable basis for estimating the probability of default. The following additional data are available: (a) collection on the installment receivable during 2020 were 3,000, and during 2021 were P2,000, and (b) on December 1, 2021, Hsensi repossessed the computer (estimated net realizable value, P7,000).`r`n`r`n

`r`nUnrealized gross profit, 2020100,000`r`n`r`n
`r`nCapital stock600,000`r`n`r`n
`r`nRetained earnings80,000`r`n`r`n
`r`nGain on repossession6,000`r`n`r`n
`r`nOperating expensesP50,000`r`n`r`n
`r`nTotal932,000932,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`nThe cost of goods sold had been uniform, over the years at 60% of sales.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`nRainbow Products Corporation adopts perpetual inventory procedures. On installment sales, the corporation charges installment accounts receivable and credits inventory gross profit accounts.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`nRepossessions of merchandise have been made during 2020 due to some customer's failure to pay maturing installments. Analyses of these transactions were summarized as follows: `r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`nInventory P7,500`r`n`r`n
`r`nUnrealized gross profit, 2018800`r`n`r`n
`r`nUnrealized gross profit, 2019 2,400`r`n`r`n
`r`nInstallment accounts receivable, 2018P2,000`r`n`r`n
`r`nInstallment accounts receivable, 2019 6,000`r`n`r`n
`r`nGain on repossession 2,700`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`nThe repossessed merchandise was unsold on December 31, 2020. It was ascertained that they were booked upon repossession at original costs. A fair valuation of these items would be a sale price of the repossessed merchandise at P 10,000 after incurring costs of reconditioning of P5,000 and cost to dispose of them in the market at P500. `r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`nWhat is the realized gross profit of 2020 sales was: (Installment Sales)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n44,000 `r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n56,000 `r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n124,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n136,000 `r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nJoel and Jonats formed a joint venture on January 1, 2013, to operate two stores to be managed by each venturer/participants. They agreed to contribute cash as follows: `r`n`r`n
`r`nJoel P30,000`r`n`r`n
`r`nJonats 20,000 `r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`nProfits and losses are to be divided in the capital ratio. All venture transactions are for cash. Cash receipt and disbursements of the business during the four months handled through the participant's venturers bank accounts are as follows: `r`n`r`n
`r`nJoelJonats`r`n`r`n
`r`nReceipts P78,920P65,425`r`n`r`n
`r`nDisbursements 62,27570,695`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`nOn April 30, the remaining non-cash venture assets in the hands of the participants/venturers were sold for P60,000. The venture is terminated and settled is made between Joel and Jonats. `r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`nThe P60,000 is divided between the participants/venturers as follows: (Joint Ventures)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nJoel (16,180); Jonats (43,820)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nJoel (21,905); Jonats (38,095) `r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nJoel (26,180); Jonats (33,820) `r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nJoel (48,095); Jonats (11,905)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n111.Ross and Reggie formed a joint venture to purchase and sell a special type of merchandise. The venturers agreed to contribute cash of P270,000 each to be used in purchasing the merchandise and to share profits and losses equally. They also agreed that each should record the purchases, sales, and expenses in their books. `r`n`r`n
`r`nUpon termination of the joint venture, the following data are made available: `r`n`r`n
`r`nRossReggie`r`n`r`n

`r`n`r`n`r`n

`r`n`r`n

`r`n113. USC, a nonprofit university, received the following cash contributions from donors during the year 2020:`r`n`r`n
`r`nUnrestricted contributionsP 250,000`r`n`r`n
`r`nContributions restricted by donors for scholarship programs 100,000`r`n`r`n
`r`nContributions from a donor who stipulated that the money be spent in`r`n`r`n
`r`naccordance with the wishes of the hospital's board of trustees 75,000`r`n`r`n
`r`nContributions restricted by donors for equipment acquisitionsP125,000`r`n`r`n
`r`nAssuming the university spent P 75,000-of the donors' contributions for scholarship programs on financing this year's scholars, how much should be included in its current fund's revenue for the year ended December 31, 2020? (Revenue Recognition)`r`n`r`n

`r`n114. Wood Den. a private, not-for-profit zoological society, received contributions restricted for`r`n`r`n
`r`nresearch totaling P40,000 in 2017. None of the contributions were spent on research in 2017. In 2020, P28,000 of the contributions were used to support the research activities of the society.`r`n`r`n
`r`nThe net effect on the statement of activities for the year ended December 31, 2020, for Wood Den would be a`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP 12,000 increase in temporarily restricted net assets.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP28,000 decrease in temporarily restricted net assets.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP28,000 increase in unrestricted net assets.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nP28,000 decrease in unrestricted net assets.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n115. St. Luke's Hospital, a nonprofit hospital-affiliated, received the following cash contributions from donors during the year ended December 31, 2020: `r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`nContributions restricted by donors for research P50,000`r`n`r`n
`r`nContributions restricted by donors for capital acquisitions 250,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`nNeither of the contributions was spent during 2020; however, during 2021, the hospital spent the entire P50,000 contribution on research and the entire P250,000 contribution on a capital asset, which was placed into service during the year. On the hospital's statement of Operations for the year ended December 31, 2021, what total amount should be reported for “net assets released from restrictions.” (Revenue Recognition)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n0`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n50,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n250,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n300,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n116. TD decided to withdraw from his partnership with SM and MR. Before his withdrawal, TD's capital balance was P 58,000, while SM's was P64,000, and MR's was P77,000. Also, the partnership's total assets amounted to P 450,000, but the partners agreed that a fixed asset was under depreciated by P 15,000. TD, SM, and MR share profits and losses in the ratio of 2:4:4, respectively, If TD was paid P53,200 upon his retirement, how much is the remaining partnership net assets after TD's withdrawal? (Partnership – Withdrawal)`r`n`r`n

Jet is to invest a sufficient amount to have a 25% interest in the partnership. If Hannah and Blanca share profit in a proportion of 3:1, respectively, and Blanca's capital balance after Jet's investment is P84,250, how much was invested by Jet? (Partnership – Admission)`r`n`r`n

The capital balances are P92,000 for Dorian and P68,000 for Donnie. `r`n`r`n

`r`n119. On January 1, 2020, A, B, and C fanned a partnership with capital contributions as follows:`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`nAP50,000`r`n`r`n
`r`nB25,000`r`n`r`n
`r`nC25,000`r`n`r`n
`r`nD20,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`nThe partnership agreement stipulates that each partner shall receive a 5% interest in capital contributed and that A and a shall receive salaries of P5,000 and P3 ,000, respectively. The agreement further provides that C shall receive a minimum of P2,500 per annum and D a minimum of P6,000 which is inclusive of amounts representing interest and their respective shares in partnership profits. The balance of the profits shall be distributed among A, B, C, and D in the ratio of 3:3:2:2.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`nWhat amount must be earned by the partnership in 2020, before any charges for interest and partners' salaries so that A may receive an aggregate of P 12,500 including interest, salary, and share of profits? (Partnership – Operations)`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n30,667`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n32,334`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n16,667`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n30,000`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n

`r`n`r`n`r`n

`r`n`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nEach partner is allocated interest equal to 10% of the beginning capital balance.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nDad a is allocated compensation of P20,000 per year.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nAny remaining profits and losses are allocated on a 3:3:4 basis, respectively.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`nEach partner is allowed to withdraw up to P5,000 cash per year.`r`n`r`n
`r`n`r`n`r`n

`r`n`r`n`r`n

`r`n197. On September 1, 2020, Bain Company received an order for equipment from a foreign customer for 300,000 local cu1Tency units (LCU) when the US dollar equivalent was P96,000. Bain shipped the equipment on October 15, 2018, and billed the customer for 300,000 LCU when the US dollar equivalent was P 100,000. Bain received the customer remittance in full on November 16, 2018, and sold the 300,000 LCU for P 105,000.
